content.text: Every agentic AI system that executes code, runs shell commands, or invokes tools on behalf of a user is also executing untrusted computation. Without explicit ceilings on what that computation can consume, a single misbehaving agent turn can exhaust the memory of an entire host, saturate CPU for minutes, fill a disk, or fork thousands of processes before the operator notices. Resource limits are the kernel-level and orchestrator-level controls that cap what any individual sandbox can take from the shared system.

Resource limits are quantitative constraints placed on a process tree — or a container — that prevent it from consuming more than a defined amount of CPU time, memory, disk space, file descriptors, processes, or network bandwidth. In the context of AI agent sandboxing, they function as the second enforcement layer after namespace and capability restrictions: namespaces control
a process can see, while resource limits control
On Linux, resource limits are implemented through two complementary mechanisms. The

syscall) sets per-process limits that are inherited by child processes and checked by the kernel on every relevant operation. The cgroup (control group) subsystem — specifically cgroup v2 on modern kernels — imposes limits on the aggregate resource consumption of an entire process hierarchy, making it effective even when agent-generated code spawns multiple processes or threads.
Container runtimes like Docker and containerd expose both mechanisms through a unified API: flags like

translate directly to cgroup v2 parameters and ulimit settings on the container's init process. This means the same high-level constraint applies uniformly regardless of whether agent-generated code is a single-threaded Python script, a multi-process Node.js app, or a shell pipeline that spawns dozens of utilities.

Without resource limits, a sandboxed code executor is only partially protected. An adversarial or buggy piece of agent-generated code that cannot escape the filesystem or network namespace can still cause severe operational damage by consuming all available memory (triggering OOM conditions on the host), saturating CPU (starving the orchestrator itself), or forking enough processes to exhaust the kernel's PID table. These are denial-of-service vectors that operate entirely within the sandbox's permitted system calls.

The threat model for agentic AI code execution is more challenging than for traditional sandboxing because the code author is an LLM responding to arbitrary user inputs. Prompt injection through external content — a webpage the agent reads, a document it processes — can produce code that deliberately attempts resource exhaustion. Even without adversarial inputs, LLM-generated code frequently has bugs: accidental infinite loops, unbounded data loading, and recursive functions without base cases. Resource limits convert these failures from host-level incidents into contained container-level errors.
— code that allocates large arrays or loads a multi-gigabyte file into memory can OOM-kill the host OS, taking down the orchestrator and all other tenant containers with it.
— an unintentional or deliberate infinite loop consumes 100% of a core indefinitely, delaying all other agent requests and degrading user experience across the system.
in a tight Python loop) can fill the kernel PID table in seconds, rendering the host unresponsive until rebooted.
in a loop can fill the host filesystem, causing every service on the machine to fail once the disk reaches capacity.
— in shared infrastructure, one user's code execution must not starve other users of CPU or memory. Resource limits enforce the fairness contract that a scheduler alone cannot guarantee.
— even when agent-generated code attempts resource exhaustion, the cgroup ceiling stops the damage at the sandbox level. The host kernel, orchestrator, and other containers remain operational.
— CPU and memory caps translate directly to predictable infrastructure cost. A 0.5 CPU, 128 MB sandbox cannot consume more than that budget, enabling accurate capacity planning.
Graceful failure instead of cascading outage

— when a container exceeds its memory limit the kernel OOM-kills that container. The orchestrator receives a non-zero exit code and returns a structured error to the agent, which can retry with simplified code. Without limits, the same overrun kills the host.
— resource limits apply regardless of what the code does: they work against bugs, against prompt-injected attacks, and against code that uses third-party libraries with unexpected resource behavior.

— declaring resource quotas in a container spec or cgroup configuration creates a machine-readable, version-controllable contract that can be reviewed by security teams and referenced in audit reports.
Limits that are too tight break legitimate workloads
— a 64 MB memory limit that works for simple arithmetic will OOM-kill a pandas DataFrame operation on a modest CSV. Finding the right balance requires profiling typical agent workloads.
CPU throttling can inflate wall-clock time unpredictably

— cgroup CPU quotas throttle the process by inserting forced idle periods. A computation that would take 1 second at full speed can take 10 seconds at 10% quota, which interacts badly with wall-clock timeouts.
Memory limits do not prevent slow leaks
— a process that leaks memory slowly may approach the limit without triggering the OOM killer for minutes, consuming steadily more memory and degrading throughput before the kill happens.

— older distributions use cgroup v1, which has a separate hierarchy per controller and different semantics. Code written for cgroup v2 (unified hierarchy) does not map directly to cgroup v1 without translation.
File descriptor limits require separate enforcement
— cgroups do not limit open file descriptors. A process can exhaust the kernel's file descriptor table, which affects the entire system. ulimit
Network bandwidth limits require a separate tool
— cgroups v2 does not throttle network bandwidth directly (only blocks/io for block devices). Traffic shaping requires
or an eBPF-based policy, adding operational complexity.

Linux enforces resource limits through two independent but complementary kernel subsystems. Understanding both is essential for configuring a sandbox correctly, because they protect against different attack shapes and are applied at different granularities.
The cgroup v2 CPU controller uses two files to control CPU allocation.
accepts two numbers: a quota (microseconds of CPU time per period) and a period (length of the period in microseconds). Setting

means the cgroup may use at most 50,000 µs of CPU in each 100,000 µs window — exactly 50% of one logical CPU. When the quota is exhausted mid-period, the kernel throttles all processes in the cgroup until the next period begins. The process is not killed; it simply stops being scheduled. This throttling is transparent to the process — it sees a slower clock, not an error.

in v1) is a softer mechanism: it sets relative scheduling priority among cgroups competing for spare CPU capacity. A weight of 100 (default) means the cgroup competes equally with others; a weight of 10 means it yields to all others when CPU is contested. Soft weights are useful for background batch workloads but provide no hard guarantee for interactive agent turns.
sets a hard ceiling on the total physical memory a cgroup's processes can use. When the process attempts an allocation that would push the cgroup over

, the kernel invokes the OOM killer, which terminates the most memory-hungry process inside the cgroup. From the orchestrator's perspective, the container exits with exit code 137 (SIGKILL from OOM killer). Setting
disables swap for the cgroup entirely, ensuring that a memory-hungry process is killed quickly rather than causing disk I/O degradation as it swaps pages.
is a softer threshold: when crossed, the kernel applies memory pressure to the cgroup, throttling allocation and triggering reclaim. It is useful as a warning threshold set below
to slow down a runaway process before the OOM killer fires. The typical pattern for agent sandboxes is to set
caps the total number of tasks (threads and processes) the cgroup can have alive simultaneously. When the limit is reached, any
(resource temporarily unavailable). The existing processes continue running; only new process creation is blocked. This is the primary defense against fork bombs.

For a typical single-language code-interpreter sandbox (Python or Node), a limit of 16–64 processes is sufficient for normal operation while blocking any exponential forking pattern. Set the limit high enough that the runtime's own worker threads (garbage collector, I/O threads) are not affected, but low enough that a fork loop never runs more than a few iterations before hitting the ceiling.
Disk resource limits have two components. First, the cgroup
controller limits read/write bytes per second (bps) and I/O operations per second (iops) to specific block devices. Setting
caps the sandbox to 10 MB/s on the device with major:minor
. This prevents a code snippet that reads a large file from saturating the shared disk.
Second, the total disk space available to the container is limited by mounting
) and making the root filesystem read-only. The container can only write to the
, and the size cap prevents it from growing larger than the specified amount. When the tmpfs fills, write syscalls return
rather than filling the host disk.

A wall-clock timeout is enforced by the orchestrator, not by the kernel. After dispatching the execution request, the orchestrator starts a timer. When the timer expires, it sends SIGKILL to the container (or tears it down via
). SIGKILL cannot be caught or ignored by user-space code, guaranteeing termination regardless of what the process is doing.

The timeout is the safety net against computations that stay within memory and CPU bounds but take too long — sorting a large array, fitting a machine learning model, performing symbolic integration. For interactive agent use cases (code interpreter in a chat interface), 15–30 seconds is the practical maximum before the user experience degrades. For batch data analysis, 5–10 minutes is reasonable. The key is setting the timeout based on the use case rather than using a single global value.
interface provides additional per-process limits that complement cgroups.
caps the number of open file descriptors; setting this to 256–1024 prevents code from exhausting the system-wide file descriptor table.
is a per-user process limit that complements cgroup
limits the maximum size of any single file a process can create, providing an additional layer of disk protection on top of the tmpfs size cap.

attrs.alt: Resource limits architecture showing an AI agent submitting code through a cgroup v2 Resource Controller to six parallel enforcement boxes: CPU Quota, Memory Limit, PID Limit, Disk I/O Limit, Network Limit, and Wall-Clock Timeout, with captured stdout returned and the container torn down.

content.text: The diagram illustrates the six enforcement dimensions that operate simultaneously on every agent code execution. The cgroup v2 controller is the single attachment point: when a container starts, all six limits are registered with the kernel before the first line of user code runs. CPU throttling and memory OOM handling are kernel-side; the wall-clock timeout is enforced by the orchestrator's own timer firing a SIGKILL at the container from outside.

Think of a resource-limited sandbox as a vending machine for computation. The user inserts a request (agent-generated code) into the slot. The machine has a fixed set of compartments: a small coin tray for CPU time, a tank for memory, a ticket roll for PIDs, a small shelf for disk writes, and a countdown clock visible on the front. The machine will dispense as much computation as these compartments can hold. When any compartment empties, the machine stops — it does not ask the house for more coins or a bigger tank. It returns whatever it has computed so far and resets for the next user.
This model captures the key insight that resource limits are

enforce a ceiling, not a suggestion. There is no mechanism for the process inside to negotiate a larger allocation, extend the timeout, or borrow from adjacent containers. When the clock hits zero, the machine shuts off regardless of whether the computation is done.
— resource limits must be applied to every sandbox that executes agent-generated code. There is no scenario where omitting them is acceptable in production.
— when multiple users share the same host, CPU and memory limits are the primary fairness mechanism. Without them, one user's heavy workload degrades everyone else.
— strict wall-clock timeouts (15–30 seconds) ensure the user never waits indefinitely. Pair with a tight CPU limit so that even a CPU-bound infinite loop will not delay the timeout response.
— looser timeouts (5–10 minutes) but still strict memory and PID limits. A batch job that loads too much data should OOM cleanly rather than swapping the host to a halt.
When NOT to apply aggressive limits

— scientific computing workloads that legitimately require large memory (fitting ML models, processing genomic data) need limits tuned to those workloads. Applying standard interactive limits to batch scientific jobs will cause constant OOM kills. The limits should be use-case-specific, not globally uniform.

The following steps describe configuring resource limits for a Docker-based agent code execution sandbox. The same cgroup v2 parameters apply when using containerd, Podman, or any OCI-compatible runtime; only the CLI flag syntax differs.
Set the memory hard limit and disable swap.
). Setting swap equal to the memory limit means the container can use the specified amount of RAM and zero swap.
to specify a fractional CPU allocation.
alone — it is a soft weight, not a hard cap.
with a value appropriate for the runtime. For single-threaded Python, 16–32 is sufficient. For runtimes that use thread pools (Node.js, JVM), 64–128 may be needed.
Make the root filesystem read-only and mount a tmpfs for writes.
. The tmpfs size is enforced by the kernel: writes past the limit return
rather than growing the host disk.
to cap open file descriptors, and
as an additional PID guard (in addition to
Enforce a wall-clock timeout in the orchestrator.

Do not rely on the container runtime's timeout flags alone. Wrap the container execution in an async context manager or a subprocess call with a timeout, and send SIGKILL via the Docker API when it expires.

Exit code 137 indicates OOM kill or SIGKILL (timeout). Exit code 1 is a Python exception or non-zero exit. Distinguish these cases in the orchestrator so the agent can respond appropriately — retry with simplified code for OOM vs. report a bug for exit code 1.

# Run agent-generated Python code with all resource limits applied.
# Adjust the values to match your use case before deploying.

docker run \
  --rm \
  --name "agent-sandbox-$(date +%s%N)" \
  # Memory: 128 MB hard limit, no swap
  --memory=128m \
  --memory-swap=128m \
  --memory-swappiness=0 \
  # CPU: 50% of one core
  --cpus=0.5 \
  # PIDs: block fork bombs
  --pids-limit=32 \
  # Filesystem: read-only root, writable tmpfs
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp:size=32m,noexec \
  # File descriptors
  --ulimit nofile=256:256 \
  --ulimit nproc=32:32 \
  # Security: no capabilities, no privilege escalation
  --cap-drop=ALL \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges:true \
  --user 1000:1000 \
  # Network: isolated (no egress)
  --network=none \
  # Image
  python:3.12-alpine \
  python /sandbox/script.py
Example 1 — Python orchestrator with resource-limited Docker execution
This function wraps a Docker container run with all resource limits and a wall-clock timeout, returning a structured result that distinguishes OOM kills from timeouts from normal exits.

import docker
import threading
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class SandboxResult:
    stdout: str
    stderr: str
    exit_code: int
    timed_out: bool
    oom_killed: bool

def run_with_limits(
    code: str,
    image: str = "python:3.12-alpine",
    memory_mb: int = 128,
    cpus: float = 0.5,
    pids: int = 32,
    tmpfs_mb: int = 32,
    timeout_seconds: int = 20,
    max_output_bytes: int = 65536,
) -> SandboxResult:
    """
    Run agent-generated Python code in a resource-limited Docker container.
    Returns a SandboxResult with outcome classification.
    """
    client = docker.from_env()

    container = client.containers.run(
        image=image,
        command=["python", "-c", code],
        detach=True,
        # Memory limits
        mem_limit=f"{memory_mb}m",
        memswap_limit=f"{memory_mb}m",   # swap = 0
        mem_swappiness=0,
        # CPU limits
        nano_cpus=int(cpus * 1_000_000_000),
        # PID limit
        pids_limit=pids,
        # Filesystem
        read_only=True,
        tmpfs={"/tmp": f"size={tmpfs_mb}m,noexec"},
        # File descriptors
        ulimits=[
            docker.types.Ulimit(name="nofile", soft=256, hard=256),
            docker.types.Ulimit(name="nproc", soft=pids, hard=pids),
        ],
        # Security
        cap_drop=["ALL"],
        security_opt=["no-new-privileges:true"],
        user="1000:1000",
        # Network
        network_mode="none",
    )

    timed_out = False
    try:
        container.wait(timeout=timeout_seconds)
    except Exception:
        timed_out = True
        try:
            container.kill()
        except Exception:
            pass

    # Reload to get final state
    container.reload()
    oom_killed = container.attrs.get("State", {}).get("OOMKilled", False)
    exit_code = container.attrs.get("State", {}).get("ExitCode", -1)

    raw_logs = container.logs(stdout=True, stderr=False)
    err_logs = container.logs(stdout=False, stderr=True)
    container.remove(force=True)

    return SandboxResult(
        stdout=raw_logs.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")[:max_output_bytes],
        stderr=err_logs.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")[:4096],
        exit_code=-1 if timed_out else exit_code,
        timed_out=timed_out,
        oom_killed=oom_killed,
    )
Example 2 — Verifying limits are applied at runtime
Before deploying to production, verify that the cgroup limits are actually visible from inside the container. This test script checks each limit dimension and reports what the kernel has set.

"""Run this script INSIDE the sandbox container to verify resource limits."""
import os
import resource
from pathlib import Path

def read_cgroup(filename: str) -> str:
    for base in ["/sys/fs/cgroup", "/sys/fs/cgroup/unified"]:
        p = Path(base) / filename
        if p.exists():
            return p.read_text().strip()
    return "(not found)"

print("=== Resource Limit Verification ===")
print(f"memory.max:      {read_cgroup('memory.max')} bytes")
print(f"memory.swap.max: {read_cgroup('memory.swap.max')}")
print(f"cpu.max:         {read_cgroup('cpu.max')}")
print(f"pids.max:        {read_cgroup('pids.max')}")

# Check ulimits
for limit_name, limit_code in [
    ("RLIMIT_NOFILE", resource.RLIMIT_NOFILE),
    ("RLIMIT_NPROC", resource.RLIMIT_NPROC),
    ("RLIMIT_FSIZE", resource.RLIMIT_FSIZE),
]:
    soft, hard = resource.getrlimit(limit_code)
    print(f"{limit_name}: soft={soft}, hard={hard}")

# Check filesystem
print(f"\n/tmp writable: {os.access('/tmp', os.W_OK)}")
print(f"/ writable:    {os.access('/', os.W_OK)}")
print(f"Running as UID: {os.getuid()}, GID: {os.getgid()}")

# Network check
try:
    import socket
    s = socket.socket()
    s.settimeout(1)
    s.connect(("8.8.8.8", 53))
    print("Network: OPEN (unexpected — check --network=none)")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Network: BLOCKED ({e})")

A SaaS platform offers an AI assistant that lets data analysts run custom SQL transformations and Python post-processing scripts against their uploaded datasets. The platform serves thousands of concurrent users and cannot afford to let any single user's script affect others. Each execution runs in a dedicated container provisioned with the following limits: 256 MB memory, 0.5 CPU, 64 PIDs, 64 MB tmpfs, and a 45-second wall-clock timeout. The memory budget was established by profiling the 99th percentile memory usage of 100,000 sample analytical queries — 256 MB covers all but the most extreme cases while remaining well below the 2 GB host limit per 8 active containers.

When a user submits a script that accidentally loads their entire 500 MB CSV into memory at once, the container is OOM-killed, the orchestrator catches exit code 137, and the agent responds: "The script ran out of memory. Try loading the data in chunks using
." The host kernel, other containers, and other users are completely unaffected. The failed container is torn down within milliseconds of the OOM event.

# orchestrator/executor.py
import docker
import json
from enum import Enum

class FailureReason(str, Enum):
    OOM = "out_of_memory"
    TIMEOUT = "timeout"
    FORK_BOMB = "too_many_processes"
    SCRIPT_ERROR = "script_error"
    SUCCESS = "success"

def classify_exit(result) -> FailureReason:
    if result["timed_out"]:
        return FailureReason.TIMEOUT
    if result["oom_killed"]:
        return FailureReason.OOM
    if result["exit_code"] == 0:
        return FailureReason.SUCCESS
    # heuristic: pids.max hit causes EAGAIN in fork, which Python
    # typically surfaces as OSError: [Errno 11] Resource temporarily unavailable
    if "Resource temporarily unavailable" in result["stderr"]:
        return FailureReason.FORK_BOMB
    return FailureReason.SCRIPT_ERROR

FAILURE_HINTS = {
    FailureReason.OOM: (
        "The script exceeded the memory limit. "
        "Try processing data in smaller chunks or reducing the dataset size."
    ),
    FailureReason.TIMEOUT: (
        "The script took longer than the allowed time. "
        "Consider optimizing the algorithm or reducing the input size."
    ),
    FailureReason.FORK_BOMB: (
        "The script spawned too many processes. "
        "Avoid recursive subprocess calls or parallel process pools."
    ),
    FailureReason.SCRIPT_ERROR: (
        "The script exited with an error. See the output for details."
    ),
}

def execute_agent_code(code: str, dataset_path: str) -> dict:
    client = docker.from_env()
    container = client.containers.run(
        image="analytics-sandbox:latest",
        command=["python", "/sandbox/script.py"],
        volumes={dataset_path: {"bind": "/data/input.csv", "mode": "ro"}},
        environment={"DATASET": "/data/input.csv"},
        detach=True,
        mem_limit="256m",
        memswap_limit="256m",
        nano_cpus=500_000_000,
        pids_limit=64,
        read_only=True,
        tmpfs={"/tmp": "size=64m,noexec"},
        network_mode="none",
        cap_drop=["ALL"],
        security_opt=["no-new-privileges:true"],
        user="1000:1000",
    )

    timed_out = False
    try:
        container.wait(timeout=45)
    except Exception:
        timed_out = True
        container.kill()

    container.reload()
    oom = container.attrs["State"]["OOMKilled"]
    code_exit = container.attrs["State"]["ExitCode"]
    stdout = container.logs(stdout=True, stderr=False).decode(errors="replace")[:65536]
    stderr = container.logs(stdout=False, stderr=True).decode(errors="replace")[:4096]
    container.remove(force=True)

    result = {
        "stdout": stdout,
        "stderr": stderr,
        "exit_code": -1 if timed_out else code_exit,
        "timed_out": timed_out,
        "oom_killed": oom,
    }
    reason = classify_exit(result)
    result["failure_reason"] = reason.value
    result["agent_hint"] = FAILURE_HINTS.get(reason, "")
    return result

to twice the memory limit, allowing the container to swap 128 MB for a 128 MB RAM limit. This means a runaway process can actually use 256 MB before being killed, and it will cause severe I/O contention. Always set
Relying on --cpu-shares (soft weight) instead of --cpus (hard quota)
only matters when CPU is contested. On an idle host, a container with
can consume 100% of all cores. Only

— this is the single most commonly omitted resource limit. Without it, a fork bomb will exhaust the kernel PID table. The default Docker daemon behavior (as of Docker 20) sets a default pids-limit of 0 (no limit) unless
. Always set it explicitly per container.
Using a tmpfs without the noexec flag
, code can write an executable to
as a staging area for binaries.
Not removing the container after execution
— containers that are not removed accumulate on the host and consume disk space for their writable layers. Always call
Setting a timeout without handling OOM exits separately
— when the orchestrator catches a container exit without checking
in the container state, it cannot distinguish a timeout from an OOM kill. Both present as SIGKILL (exit 137). Check
after the container exits to provide the right feedback to the agent.
Applying the same limits to all workloads

— a 128 MB limit is right for simple arithmetic but wrong for loading a 100 MB CSV with pandas. Consider offering tiered execution profiles (e.g., "fast": 128 MB / 15 s, "standard": 512 MB / 60 s, "batch": 2 GB / 10 min) and routing agent requests to the appropriate tier based on declared intent.
Profile real workloads before setting limits.

Collect memory, CPU, and duration distributions from 10,000+ representative agent code executions before choosing the default limits. Set the hard limit at the 99th percentile plus a 25% buffer, not at an arbitrary round number.
Set memory.high as a soft warning threshold.
via the cgroup filesystem. Processes crossing
are slowed down and their allocations are throttled, giving the Python garbage collector a chance to reclaim before an OOM kill.
Monitor OOM kill rates and adjust.

Log every OOM kill event to your observability platform. If the OOM rate exceeds 0.5% of executions, the memory limit is likely too tight for the workload mix. A rate of 0% may indicate the limits are too loose (no benefit) or the workload is well-behaved.
Use pre-warmed container pools to amortize startup overhead.

Container startup (cold-starting Docker, loading the Python interpreter) takes 50–300 ms. For interactive agent use, keep a pool of pre-started containers waiting for code to run. After each execution, discard the used container and start a fresh one to replenish the pool.
Never allow limit parameters to be user-controlled.

The orchestrator should select the execution tier from a fixed set of profiles. User-supplied values for memory limits, timeouts, or CPU quotas open a privilege escalation vector where a user requests a 16 GB container.
Configure the Docker daemon's default limits as a safety net.
so that any container inadvertently started without explicit
Test every limit dimension with a synthetic attack.

Write test cases that deliberately trigger each limit: an allocation loop for memory, a busy-wait for CPU, a recursive fork for PIDs, a write loop for disk, and a sleep loop for timeout. Confirm that each produces the expected exit code and takes no longer than the limit allows.

The performance overhead of cgroup v2 resource limits is near-zero for well-behaved processes. The kernel checks memory limits on every allocation, but these checks are already part of the normal page-fault path and add no measurable overhead at steady state. CPU quota enforcement adds a periodic check (every 100 ms by default) to account CPU usage and insert throttle periods; this check is O(1) and runs in kernel context, invisible to user-space.

The latency cost that matters for production sizing is not limit enforcement but container lifecycle: image pull (one-time, cached), container start (50–200 ms for namespace and cgroup setup), Python interpreter startup (50–100 ms inside the container), and container removal (10–50 ms). For an interactive agent turn targeting a 1-second P99 response time, the available budget for user code execution is roughly 600–800 ms after accounting for these overheads plus the LLM inference time. Pre-warmed pools reduce the container start overhead to near-zero by paying it upfront.

Horizontal scaling of sandboxed code execution is straightforward because each execution is stateless and ephemeral. The limiting factor is usually memory: at 128 MB per container slot, a 16 GB host supports about 100 concurrent active containers with headroom for the OS and orchestrator. With 512 MB slots for heavier workloads, that drops to about 25. Plan capacity based on concurrency targets and memory slot size, not CPU. CPU limits are per-container and the kernel's fair scheduler handles contention automatically.
Translates flags to cgroup v2 writes; widely supported
Direct kernel interface; most expressive; requires root or CAP_SYS_ADMIN
No container runtime needed; transient scope cleaned up automatically
CPU, memory per pod; eviction policies
Use LimitRange to set namespace-wide defaults; combine with PodDisruptionBudget
File descriptors, file size, process count
POSIX setrlimit; applied per-process; does not aggregate across threads
CPU, memory, vCPU count at VM level
Stronger isolation than containers; resource limits apply to the microVM
Managed Firecracker microVMs; resource limits configured per sandbox template
Separate hierarchy per controller; complex setup
Only what the app explicitly checks; bypassed by ctypes, native libs
vCPU, RAM, disk at VM level; strongest but highest startup cost
Application-level resource budgeting — where the Python script checks its own memory usage with

or tracks its own execution time — provides no security guarantee because agent-generated code controls the check. A process can simply not call the check, or call native code that bypasses it. Kernel-enforced limits (cgroups, ulimits) are mandatory; application-level checks are, at best, a courtesy for user-facing error messages.

Resource limits are a correctness control as much as a security control. From a security perspective, they prevent denial-of-service attacks where prompt-injected code attempts to exhaust host resources. From a reliability perspective, they prevent accidental self-inflicted outages where a buggy agent turn takes down the host. Both threat models are active in production agentic AI systems.

The reliability concern is arguably more common than the security concern in practice: LLM-generated code that accidentally loads a large dataset, recurses infinitely, or opens a file in a loop is far more frequent than deliberately adversarial code. Resource limits catch both cases with the same mechanism, which makes them uniquely cost-effective as a safety control.

— write automated tests that attempt to exceed each resource dimension and assert the expected exit code and error message. This prevents limits from being inadvertently removed or misconfigured during deployments.
in cgroup v2. High sustained pressure on a cgroup before an OOM kill indicates the limit should be raised, or the workload class should be routed to a higher-tier profile.
Treat exit code 137 as a structured event, not an error

— an OOM kill or timeout kill is a normal operational event in a well-designed sandbox. The orchestrator should handle it gracefully, log it with context, and give the agent a clear diagnostic rather than surfacing a generic "internal error" to the user.
Layer resource limits with other sandbox controls

— resource limits do not replace seccomp filtering, capability dropping, or network isolation. They are additive. A process that cannot exhaust memory is still dangerous if it can open a network connection. All sandbox controls must be applied together.
Use LimitRange in Kubernetes for fleet-wide enforcement
object in the sandbox namespace enforces default and maximum resource requests/limits for every pod, ensuring that a misconfigured deployment descriptor cannot create an unlimited pod.

Resource limits prevent agent-generated code from causing host-level damage through memory exhaustion, CPU starvation, fork bombs, or disk flooding — all within the boundary of otherwise permitted system calls.
cgroup v2 is the primary enforcement mechanism on modern Linux:
blocks forking past the ceiling. All are enforced by the kernel and cannot be bypassed by user-space code.
to disable swap, and always set
explicitly — these are the two most commonly omitted limit flags and the two most consequential omissions.

A wall-clock timeout enforced by the orchestrator is mandatory in addition to cgroup limits — it is the only defense against computations that run slowly but stay within memory and CPU bounds indefinitely.
and make the root filesystem read-only — this limits disk consumption and prevents code from staging executable binaries in the writable area.
OOM kills (exit 137) and timeouts are normal, expected operational events in a production sandbox. Handle them as structured outcomes with specific agent feedback, not as generic errors.
Calibrate limits against real workload distributions, not arbitrary round numbers. Profile 99th percentile memory and duration, add a margin, and revisit the limits as workload patterns evolve.

Resource limits are additive with other sandbox controls — they do not replace seccomp filtering, capability dropping, or network isolation. A complete sandbox applies all of these layers simultaneously.
